channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.rockpapershotgun.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.rockpapershotgun.com

item.title: Moonlighter dev's next haunt is ReVamp, a tower defence roguelike where Dracula is cursed to defend his castle forever
item.content:encoded:
The devs behind Moonlighter are already back with something new. While they're still tinkering away with the early access release of Moonlighter 2, at the Future Games Show Spring Showcase they revealed another roguelike entirely: ReVamp, a tower defence spin on the genre that pits you as Dracula himself protecting his castle.

Now, you might be wondering why Dracula is stuck having to defend his castle. That's because he's been cursed by Achlys, goddess of the night, forcing him (and subsequently you) to face endless waves of mortals every night. Rather than Dracula himself fending off his foes, you can place various monsters, beasties, skeletons and the like all with their own abilities and such.

The biggest twist on it all is that the castle itself is entirely modular. Between onslaughts, you can add in new rooms, move them around, and position them in ways that will best result in the deaths of your foes. The more mortals you kill, the more blood you get, which you can then re-invest into your castle to improve the various rooms at your disposal, all of which have their own unique effects that'll help you in the fight too.

Oh, and you can actually play as Dracula too! I'm not entirely sure if you control him yourself, but the game's Steam page suggests as much. Though, this is meant to be more of a last resort when "your legion wavers," so, it won't be the main way you'll spend your time. And much like in Hades, defeat isn't the end, as between cycles you can buy new chambers, unlock better and badder creatures, and "access deeper layers of strategic possibility."

It looks neat! But it's also probably a ways away as there's no release date in sight, not even a release year. So, in the meantime, you can wishlist it on Steam right here.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/moonlighter-devs-next-haunt-is-revamp-a-tower-defence-roguelike-where-dracula-is-cursed-to-defend-his-castle-forever
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Hazard Pay is a block-pushing puzzle game you can really sink your mop into
item.content:encoded:
Video games are such funny things, because I think if you were to tell someone unfamiliar with them that there is genuine enjoyment to be had in pushing some blocks around in a virtual space they'd look at you as if you'd told them there's genuine enjoyment to be hand in pushing some blocks around in a virtual space. And yet it's true! It's one of the classic puzzle game genres, going way back to 1982 with Sokoban. And here we are now, many years later, with a new entry to the genre in the form of Hazard Pay, a dystopian take that sees you cleaning up mysterious messes in secret labs.

These messes that you have to clean up come in various forms. For example, there might be a patch of hazardous material that you have to guide towards a vat of acid. Moving around that material will spread more waste (blood) on the floor, however, and so you need to use your tools (a mop and bucket) to clean up after yourself, preferably using them to move the bits and bobs towards the vat to clean as you go. Tools like your mop and bucket and the hazardous material can be touched no problem, and can move one another, but you can't touch the vat directly, unless you put a lid on it, which there may not be in every level.

This gets ramped up in difficulty in various ways, like an annoyingly placed chair, or purposefully awkwardly laid out levels that mean you really have to think about how you're navigating the space. It also mixes things up by presenting you with just loose shirts and underwear that you can sort of fuse together to mop up the aforementioned blood. Amongst all of this, you also have to make sure you load up any waste onto your truck, as well as your tools if you have them, so it's not enough to just clean up after yourself.

It's a neat little puzzle game that is a lot more show than it is tell, with little tutorialisation shoved down your throat unnecessarily. Though, perhaps a little bit of an obvious nudge that you can hold down R to reset a level quickly would have saved me having to exit and re-enter the level more times than I'd like to admit.

Still, if any of this sounds up your alley, the demo is there for you to try out on Steam too, which you can check out right here.



https://www.rockpapershotgun.com/hazard-pay-is-a-block-pushing-puzzle-game-you-can-really-sink-your-mop-into
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
